pattern: Breaking an integer into as many threes as possible maximises the product, with a leftover of one folded into a final factor of four.
hints: Splitting a large part further always helps until the parts get small, so the optimal pieces are tiny.
Pieces of size three multiply more efficiently than any other size; pieces of two are only used to absorb a remainder.
For n above four, take out threes greedily; if the remainder is one, replace a three-plus-one with two twos, otherwise multiply in the leftover two.

edge_inputs.description: smallest input must split into two ones
three is forced to split into one and two
four splits into two twos, not three plus one
statement example using threes and a two
remainder of one folds into a four

stress.description: sweep every value across the full allowed range one at a time
stress.note: tiny domain; randomised single value within bounds
